data.title: Citigroup to cut 3,500 jobs at China tech centers
Global energy investment to touch record $3.3 trillion in 2025, IEA says
data.description:
Global energy investment is projected to reach a record $3.3 trillion in 2025, driven primarily by a surge in clean energy spending, according to a new International Energy Agency (IEA) report. The figure is a 2% rise in real terms compared to 2024, despite ongoing economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions worldwide.

About $2.2 trillion will be invested in clean energy technologies, which include renewables, nuclear, energy storage, which is twice the amount for fossil fuels.

Solar power is the largest beneficiary, with investments forecasted to hit $450 billion in 2025, making it the top energy technology globally in terms of capital allocation. Investment in battery storage is expected to reach around $65-66 billion. Capital flows to nuclear power have grown by 50% over the past five years and are on course to reach around $75 billion in 2025.

In contrast, investment in oil and gas is expected to decline, with upstream oil investment set to fall by 6% in 2025.

Spending on electricity grids is projected to surpass $400 billion in 2025 but is still lagging the pace of growth in generation and electrification. Maintaining electricity security would require investment in grids to rise towards parity with generation spending by the early 2030s.

“Amid the geopolitical and economic uncertainties that are clouding the outlook for the energy world, we see energy security coming through as a key driver of the growth in global investment this year to a record $3.3 trillion as countries and companies seek to insulate themselves from a wide range of risks,” said IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol. 

“The fast-evolving economic and trade picture means that some investors are adopting a wait-and-see approach to new energy project approvals, but in most areas we have yet to see significant implications for existing projects.”

Despite the positive trends, there are challenges; spending patterns vary globally, with many developing economies struggling to mobilize capital for energy infrastructure.

Here is a list of ETFs tracking the energy sector: (NYSEARCA:USO), (NYSEARCA:BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (NYSEARCA:SCO), (NYSEARCA:USL), (NYSEARCA:DBO), (NYSEARCA:DRIP), (NYSEARCA:GUSH), (NASDAQ:USOI), (NYSEARCA:UNG), (NYSEARCA:BOIL), (NYSEARCA:KOLD), (NYSEARCA:UNL), (NYSEARCA:FCG).

And here is a list tracking clean energy: (NASDAQ:ICLN), (NASDAQ:QCLN), (NYSEARCA:PBW), (NYSEARCA:LCTD), (NASDAQ:NZAC), (NYSEARCA:SMOG), (NYSEARCA:CNRG), (NYSEARCA:SOLR).

data.title: Five Below outlines $4.33B–$4.42B sales target amid tariff mitigation and strategic sourcing shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Below (FIVE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Winifred Y. Park opened the call highlighting that "we had a strong first quarter with financial results that exceeded our expectations," attributing outperformance to a "heightened focus on the customer and working as a tight knit multidisciplinary team from merchandising, planning and allocations to marketing, store operations and supply chain." Park emphasized broad-based sales outperformance across product categories, citing product innovation in areas such as trend-right beauty, novelty, food and candy, and tech accessories.

  • Park stated, "Sales and comps exceeded our updated guidance with sales of $971 million and comparable sales increase of 7.1%." She noted a 6.2% rise in transactions, supported by 55 new store openings in 20 states. Two openings, in Victorville, California and Joplin, Missouri, ranked among the top 25 all-time grand openings for the company.

  • The company continued investing in store experience, adding labor and improving processes, which Park said were "paying off" and positioning stores to "assist customers while also ensuring our shelves are stocked with trend-right products."

  • Park addressed the tariff environment, stating, "Our plans include vendor negotiations, diversification of sourcing, continued investment in new value pack product as well as assortment and pricing adjustments with a focus on reducing the number of price points." She highlighted a "reduction in goods sourced from China by about 10 percentage points for the back half of the year."

  • CFO Kristy Chipman remarked, "Total sales in the first quarter of 2025 increased 19.5% to $970.5 million from $811.9 million in the first quarter last year. Comparable sales increased 7.1%, driven by an increase in comp transactions of 6.2% and comp ticket of 0.9%." Chipman announced her departure for personal reasons, with an interim CFO appointed pending a national search for her replacement.

Outlook

  • COO Kenneth R. Bull provided guidance reflecting tariff impacts and current performance. "For the second quarter of 2025, we expect total sales in the range of $975 million to $995 million or growth of 18.7% at the midpoint versus last year's second quarter. Comparable sales are expected to increase between 7% and 9%."

  • Adjusted diluted earnings per share for Q2 is expected between $0.50 and $0.62. For the full year, "sales are expected to be in the range of $4.33 billion to $4.42 billion, with a comparable sales increase of 3% to 5%." Bull noted, "The midpoint of our full year operating margin guidance is unchanged from our prior outlook at approximately 7.3%."

  • The impact of tariffs is expected to largely offset improved sales performance in operating profit, with mitigation efforts ongoing.

Financial Results

  • Sales for Q1 2025 were $970.5 million, with comparable sales up 7.1%. Adjusted gross profit was $328.4 million, up 24.6%. Adjusted gross margin rose to 33.8%, an increase of approximately 140 basis points. Adjusted SG&A for the quarter was 27.7% of sales.

  • Adjusted operating income reached $59.6 million and adjusted net income was $47.5 million, resulting in adjusted diluted earnings per share of $0.86. The company ended the quarter with approximately $624 million in cash and no debt.

  • Inventory was $702 million, with per-store inventory down about 2% year-over-year due to prior write-downs. Inventory levels are expected to be "significantly higher" at the end of Q2 as the company accelerates receipts in response to global trade shifts.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS, asked about the drivers behind the "remarkable pivot in the comp cadence." Park credited "the actions that we've taken are definitively paying off," citing product selection, value, inventory flow, and improved store labor.

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, queried the source of comp strength. Park pointed to "nice growth, both in terms of new customers and comp stores as well as existing customers returning to us." She highlighted gains in beauty, food, novelty candy, style, and collectibles. Bull added, "We're going to continue to invest in the stores...hours for the stores or the simplification and reduction of workloads."

  • Charles Grom, Gordon Haskett, asked about operating margin compression and tariffs. Bull clarified, "on a full year basis in terms of the impact of tariffs, net of any mitigation activities. We see it as about 150 basis points for the full year."

  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, asked about pricing and mitigation. Park stated, "We are looking at touching about 15% in terms of price, both up and down. And it's not just for tariffs. It's really looking at relative value in the marketplace." Bull explained, "the assumption there is that any benefit that we would get from pricing adjustments would be offset by unit degradation."

  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo, inquired about margin recapture. Park emphasized, "sales solve problems," adding that tariff challenges have "accelerated work that we're doing on the merchandising front."

  • Multiple questions focused on sourcing diversification and the impact of tariffs, with Park noting a 10 percentage point reduction in China sourcing for the back half and stating, "mitigation efforts and initiatives are embedded in the guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin pressure, tariff mitigation, and sustainability of sales momentum, with a tone that is cautiously optimistic but probing, especially on the ability to sustain the comp gains and manage cost headwinds.

  • Management maintained a confident, disciplined tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently citing "strategy," "mitigation," and "execution," but also displayed prudence, particularly in guiding for the back half of the year. Park stated, "We are looking at 2-year stack comps across the next upcoming quarters of plus 2%." Slight caution was evident regarding macroeconomic uncertainty.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed greater confidence regarding sales momentum, though both sides remain wary of margin risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year sales was raised from a previous range of $4.21 billion–$4.33 billion to $4.33 billion–$4.42 billion, with comp sales expectations now 3%–5%, up from flat to 3% previously. Operating margin guidance remains steady at approximately 7.3%.

  • The company reported a sharp improvement in Q1 comparable sales (7.1% growth compared to the prior quarter’s negative comp), with strong transaction growth.

  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q4 to increased confidence in Q1, especially around customer engagement and product performance. Analysts continued to focus on margin headwinds, tariffs, and the sustainability of gains, but acknowledged improved execution.

  • The CFO transition was announced this quarter, with Kristy Chipman departing for personal reasons and an interim CFO stepping in while a search is underway.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs remain a major challenge, with management outlining mitigation strategies such as vendor negotiations, sourcing diversification, and pricing adjustments.

  • Operating margin faces pressure from tariffs (estimated at 150 basis points for the year), higher incentive compensation, and continued investments in store labor.

  • Inventory management and the ability to maintain in-stock positions amid supply chain shifts are ongoing concerns.

  • Macro uncertainty and potential consumer demand shifts were cited as reasons for prudence in guidance for the back half of the year.

Final Takeaway

Five Below’s Q1 2025 results highlight strong sales and comp performance driven by customer-focused execution, product innovation, and improved store experience. Management raised full-year sales guidance to $4.33 billion–$4.42 billion and expects comparable sales growth of 3%–5%, while maintaining operating margin targets despite significant tariff headwinds. Mitigation strategies are in place to address sourcing and cost pressures, and the company is navigating leadership changes with a new CFO search underway. The focus remains on sustaining transaction growth, expanding market presence, and delivering value amid a dynamic macro and trade environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump signs travel ban for citizens from 12 countries, citing security concerns
Thursday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:30 AM Challenger Job Cut Report  This monthly report counts and categorizes announcements of corporate layoffs based on mass layoff data from state departments. 8:30 AM Jobless Claims  New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time.  Claims are expected to fall to 235K versus 240K last week.

8:30 AM International Trade in Goods and Services

Updating the goods portion of the advance report and offering initial data on services, this report provides complete information on cross-border trade. Forecasters look for goods & services trade gap at $118.1 billion in April versus $140.5 billion in March.

8:30 AM Productivity and Costs

Productivity and costs are economic indicators that measure the efficiency of a company's production process and the cost of producing goods and services.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report

The Energy Information Administration Natural Gas Storage report measures the change in the number of cubic feet of natural gas held in underground storage over the prior week. US utilities expect 111 billion cubic feet of gas into storage during the week ended May 30, 2025.

12:00 PM Fed's Kugler Speech 

Federal Reserve Board Governor Adriana Kugler speaks on the economic outlook and monetary policy before an Economic Club of New York luncheon.

1:30 PM Fed's Harker: Economic Outlook

Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Patrick Harker speaks on the economic outlook before the Philadelphia Council for Business Economics.

1:30 PM Fed's Schmid Speech

Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid speaks on banking policy before"The Future of Banking: Navigating Change" conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City.

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed balance sheet is a weekly report listing the assets and liabilities of the Federal Reserve System.

data.title: Helius Medical Technologies stock falls after pricing capital raise of $9.1M via securities offering
Volaris reports May 2025 load factor of 82%
data.description:
Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación or Volaris (NYSE:VLRS), on Wednesday, reported its May 2025 preliminary traffic results.

In May, Volaris’ ASM capacity increased by 9.0% year-over-year, while RPMs for the month grew by 3.5%.

Mexican domestic RPMs increased 5.7%, while international RPMs were effectively flat.

As a result, the load factor decreased by 4.3 percentage points year-over-year to 81.8%. During the month, Volaris transported 2.5 million passengers.

Enrique Beltranena, Volaris’ President and CEO, said: "May’s capacity growth aligns with the adjustments outlined during our earnings call, allowing us to better match customer demand.... As we move through the second quarter, we remain aligned with our guidance for the quarter and are seeing encouraging booking trends heading into the summer high season."

data.title: Asia markets mixed on disappointing economic data, US-China trade tensions; U.S. futures flat after soft session
data.description:
Asia stock markets trade mixed on Thursday as US stocks struggled for momentum overnight, investors shifted toward safer assets amid disappointing China's PMI data and rising US-China trade tensions.

Moreover, tensions intensified over Beijing’s reluctance to relax its restrictions on rare earth exports and its accusation that Washington is obstructing access to advanced technologies.

President Donald Trump on Wednesday lashed out at Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, demanding rate cuts just after ADP reported the weakest private sector job growth since March 2023. On Truth Social, Trump wrote, “Europe has lowered nine times!”.

On the geopolitical front, trade developments also drew attention as Trump called it “extremely hard” to deal with Xi, despite earlier suggestions that the two leaders could speak this week, while China eyes a major Airbus deal in a shift toward closer EU ties.

Gold held its recent gains near $3,370 per ounce on Thursday, remaining close to a nearly four-week high as investors grappled with growing economic uncertainty. 

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.47% to around 37,700 while the broader Topix Index dropped 0.6% to 2,770 on Thursday, pressured by concerns over the country’s economic outlook after data showed real wages declined for the fourth straight month in April. The Japanese yen held firm around 142.9 on Thursday, after gaining nearly 1% in the previous session.

Japan's economic outlook faces deeper concerns as April marked the fourth consecutive decline in real wages, nominal wages in Japan rose 2.3% year-on-year in April 2025, matching the pace seen in March but falling short of market expectations of a 2.6% increase. 

The Bank of Japan is thinking about slowing bond purchase tapering next fiscal year to avoid market issues, with a decision expected at the June policy meeting, Reuters reported, citing four sources.

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.08% were little changed on Thursday, struggling for clear direction amid heightened uncertainty on Sino-US trade relations, and the offshore yuan weakened toward 7.18 per dollar on Thursday, snapping a two-session wining streak, as investors weighed disappointing PMI data and the ongoing US-China trade tensions.

A private survey of data indicating a rebound in China’s services activity in May, following a seven-month low in April. China’s composite PMI fell to a 2-1/2-year low in May 2025, marking the first contraction in private sector activity since December 2022.

China has launched a national tracking system for its rare earth magnet sector, sources told Reuters, adding a new layer of oversight as recent export restrictions begin to impact global supply chains.

Meanwhile, the OECD maintained its forecast for China’s GDP growth at 4.7%.

Meanwhile, uncertainties surrounding US-China trade relations grew after President Donald Trump emphasized the need for direct talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping to prevent further deterioration in trade and technology ties.

Hong Kong (HSI) rose 0.52% to 23,869 in Thursday morning deals, increasing for the third session.

India (SENSEX) rose 0.45% 

Australia (AS51) fell 0.06% rose 0.3% to above 8,560 on Thursday, extending its rally to a third consecutive session. The Australian dollar held its recent gains around $0.649 on Thursday.

Meanwhile, Australia’s trade surplus narrowed to AUD 5.41 billion in April, below expectations and down from a revised AUD 6.89 billion in March. The decline was driven by a 2.4% fall in exports and a 1.1% rise in imports.

In the U.S., on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended mostly muted as investors digested a steep slowdown in private-sector hiring, with the ADP report showing just 37K new jobs in May, well below expectations.

Investors cautiously awaited the latest weekly jobless claims due Thursday and the closely watched nonfarm payrolls report set for release Friday. 

U.S. stock futures dipped on Thursday after the OECD recently lowered its 2025 U.S. growth forecast to 1.6% from 2.2%, citing tariff risks and policy uncertainty: Dow -0.04%; S&P 500 -0.04%; Nasdaq -0.03%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

China's services sector growth accelerates in May amid stronger business & activity

Australia's Q1 GDP misses estimates amid weak public spending, consumer demand, and exports

Australia's manufacturing stalls at -23.5 in May, services sector continues 16-month growth

Japan services PMI revised upward to 51.0 in May 2025

data.title: Australia's trade surplus narrows in April to AUD 5.41B on weaker exports, higher imports
data.description:
Australia’s trade surplus narrowed to AUD 5.41 billion in April, below expectations and down from a revised AUD 6.89 billion in March and below market expectations of AUD 5.90 billion. The decline was driven by a 2.4% fall in exports and a 1.1% rise in imports. 

Australia's goods exports dropped 2.4% month-over-month to AUD 44.08 billion in April 2025, reversing a downwardly revised 7.2% rise in March, as shipments to the US were dampened by Trump's tariffs. 

Australia’s goods imports rose 1.1% month-over-month to AUD 38.66 billion in April 2025, rebounding from a revised 2.4% decline in March, partly due to U.S. President Trump’s 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs. 

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose 0.3% to above 8,560 on Thursday, extending its rally to a third consecutive session. The Australian dollar held its recent gains around $0.649 on Thursday.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWA), (NYSEARCA:FXA), (NYSEARCA:FLAU).

  • RBA Minutes: Rate cut signals caution, data to drive future policy

  • Australia's trade surplus shrinks to 2020 low; imports surge, exports fall

  • Australia’s monthly CPI eased to three-month low of 2.4% in February
